title: EEG Study of Interoceptive Precision Gating
description: This protocol examines whether interoceptive precision (Πⁱ), indexed by the heartbeat-evoked potential (HEP), modulates conscious detection thresholds (θₜ). Stimuli are time-locked to cardiac phase to test the prediction that high Πⁱ lowers θₜ for near-threshold visual stimuli, with the HEP (250–400 ms window) serving as a trial-by-trial proxy for Πⁱ and the P3b (300–600 ms) serving as a proxy for ignition (Bₜ). The study tests three sub-predictions: that HEP amplitude predicts P3b latency and amplitude (P1a), that cardiac-phase-locked detection advantage exists at diastole (P1b), and that the HEP–P3b relationship survives covariate control for autonomic arousal (P1c).

sub_predictions.claim: High Πⁱ lowers θₜ for salient stimuli
sub_predictions.confirming_evidence: HEP amplitude predicts P3b latency and amplitude across conditions
Cardiac-phase-locked hit-rate advantage at diastole relative to systole
sub_predictions.falsifying_evidence: Uniform detection rates across cardiac phases

sub_predictions.claim: HEP predicts detection independently of arousal
sub_predictions.confirming_evidence: HEP–P3b partial correlation survives HRV covariate control
sub_predictions.falsifying_evidence: HEP effect disappears after arousal (HRV, pupil) covariate control

validation_notes: Formally specified. Parameter identifiability confirmed via simulation (Appendix A.4). Identifiability of Πⁱ from HEP confirmed via block-diagonal Fisher Information Matrix.

inclusion_criteria: Resting heart rate 50–100 BPM with regular sinus rhythm
No history of cardiac arrhythmia or autonomic neuropathy
exclusion_criteria: Irregular ECG rhythm (assessed by study nurse at screening)
History of neurological or psychiatric disorder (DSM-5 criteria)
Use of beta-blockers or other HR-modifying medication
Inability to maintain fixation for 30-minute EEG blocks

apparatus.EEG: 64-channel BrainAmp DC (Brain Products GmbH) at 1000 Hz, referenced to FCz, re-referenced offline to average mastoids
apparatus.ECG: BrainAmp ExG bipolar lead-II at 1000 Hz, synchronised to EEG clock
apparatus.display: VPixx ProPixx at 120 Hz, viewing distance 57 cm, gamma-corrected
apparatus.stimulus_delivery: Psychtoolbox-3 (MATLAB R2024b) with photodiode verification of onset timing
apparatus.cardiac_phase_algorithm: R-peak detection via Pan-Tompkins (real-time), diastole window 300–500 ms post R-peak, systole window 0–150 ms post R-peak

trial_structure.response: 2AFC (seen / not seen), response window 1500 ms, response-locked to keypress

blocks.condition_counterbalancing: Cardiac phase pseudo-randomised across trials within block

eeg_analysis_pipeline.ica_components: Automated rejection of cardiac and ocular ICA components (EEGLAB ADJUST)

dependent_variables: Detection rate per cardiac phase (systole vs. diastole)
d-prime (signal detection theory) per cardiac phase
HEP mean amplitude (250–400 ms) at Cz, FCz, Fz
P3b mean amplitude (300–600 ms) at Pz
primary_hypothesis: HEP amplitude in the 250–400 ms window predicts P3b amplitude on a trial-by-trial basis, and detection rate is higher at diastole than systole, consistent with elevated Πⁱ_eff during cardiac deceleration lowering θₜ.
primary_analysis: Mixed-effects model: P3b_amplitude ~ HEP_amplitude * cardiac_phase + RMSSD + (HEP_amplitude * cardiac_phase | participant). Secondary: logistic mixed-effects for detection ~ cardiac_phase * contrast + (cardiac_phase | participant).

power_analysis.based_on: Garfinkel et al. (2014) cardiac-phase detection effect; Canales-Johnson et al. (2020) HEP–P3b coupling
